critique.summary: The article discusses best practices for managing AI-generated code in software development, emphasizing the importance of intentional design to maintain code quality. It introduces concepts like semantic functions, pragmatic functions, and data models to help structure codebases that remain scalable and maintainable despite AI involvement.
mainPoints: AI coding agents can rapidly degrade code quality if not managed intentionally.
Semantic functions should be minimal, self-documenting, and free of side effects to ensure correctness and reusability.
Pragmatic functions wrap semantic functions and handle complex processes but are expected to evolve and may require documentation.
Data models should enforce correctness by preventing invalid states and using precise naming and composition.
Common issues arise when semantic functions become pragmatic functions or when models accumulate unrelated fields, leading to codebase fragility.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides sound software engineering principles consistent with established best practices in code design and maintainability. Concepts like minimizing side effects, clear function responsibilities, and strict data modeling are widely recommended in professional software development. The discussion about AI coding agents reflects a reasonable caution about automated code generation but does not make unverifiable claims. Since the article is authored by an individual developer and does not cite external sources, its content aligns with accepted programming wisdom but lacks corroboration from multiple perspectives or formal studies.
considerations: The article does not address specific challenges or limitations unique to AI-generated code beyond general software design principles.
It lacks discussion on tools or methodologies to enforce these patterns automatically in AI-assisted development environments.
There is no mention of empirical evidence or case studies demonstrating the impact of these practices on AI-generated codebases.
Potential trade-offs between strict semantic function design and practical development speed with AI assistance are not explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the impact of AI on codebases, emphasizing the importance of intentionality when integrating AI-generated code. Commenters highlight concerns about optional fields, consistency erosion, and the need for human oversight and documentation.

comments.author: xiaolu627
comments.text: What changed for me isn’t that AI writes bad code by default, but that it lowers the friction to adding code faster than the team can properly absorb it. The dangerous part is not obvious bugs, it’s subtle erosion of consistency.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully identifies a subtle but significant risk of AI in coding: rapid code additions can undermine team coherence and consistency, which is a well-recognized challenge.

comments.author: AgentOrange1234
comments.text:
"Every optional field is a question the rest of the codebase has to answer every time it touches that data,"

This is a beautiful articulation of a major pet peeve when using these coding tools. One of my first review steps is just looking for all the extra optional arguments it's added instead of designing something good.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately points out that excessive optional fields complicate code maintenance, a common issue when AI-generated code is not carefully reviewed.

comments.author: benswerd
comments.text:
I've seen a lot of people talking about how AI is making codebases worse. I reject that, people are making codebases worse by not being intentional about how their AI writes code.

This is my take on how to not write slop.
comments.analysis: This comment emphasizes personal responsibility and intentionality in using AI tools, which aligns with best practices for integrating AI into development workflows.

comments.author: earljwagner
comments.text:
The concepts of Semantic Functions and Pragmatic Functions seem to be analogous to a Functional Core and Imperative shell (FCIS):

https://testing.googleblog.com/2025/10/simplify-your-code-fu...

The key insight of FCIS is that complicated logic with large dependencies leads to a large test suite that runs slowly. The solution is to isolate the complicated logic in the functional core. Test that separately from the simpler, more sequential tests of the imperative shell.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a thoughtful analogy to established software design principles, offering a constructive framework for managing complexity in AI-assisted code.

comments.author: ChrisMarshallNY
comments.text:
Because of the way that I use AI, I am constantly looking at the code. I usually leave it alone, if I can; even if I don't really like it.

I will, often go back, after the fact, and ask for refactors and documentation.

It works. Probably a lot slower than using agents, but I test every step, and it is a lot faster than I would do it, unassisted.
comments.analysis: This comment describes a practical and cautious approach to using AI-generated code, emphasizing review and iterative improvement.

comments.author: abcde666777
comments.text: My intentionality is that I'll never let it make the changes. I make the changes. I might make changes it suggests, but only upon review and only written with my hands.
comments.analysis: This comment underscores the importance of human control and review in AI-assisted coding, a widely recommended practice.

comments.author: mattacular
comments.text: Code cannot and should not be self documenting at scale. You cannot document "the why" with code. In my experience, that is only ever used as an excuse not to write actual documentation or use comments thoughtfully in the codebase by lazy developers.
comments.analysis: This comment critically addresses the limitations of code as documentation and advocates for proper documentation practices, which is a valid and important point.

comments.author: ares623
comments.analysis: This comment raises a relevant question about responsibility and control when working with AI-generated code in shared or unfamiliar codebases.

comments.author: gravitronic
comments.text:
*adds "be intentional" to the prompt*

Got it, good idea.
comments.analysis: This brief comment acknowledges the article's advice to be intentional but adds little substantive analysis.

comments.author: mrbluecoat
comments.text: ..but unintentional AI (aka Modern Chaos Monkey) is so much more fun!
comments.analysis: This comment is more humorous and less substantive, offering a playful take rather than a serious contribution.

comments.author: clbrmbr
comments.text:
Page not rendering well on iPhone Safari.

Good content tho!
comments.analysis: This comment is unrelated to the discussion about AI and codebases and thus adds no analytical value.
